uncoveredGaps: Per-row sharing/ACL (gap 3 explicitly lists 'sharing'): authz F5 is 'RECOMMEND DEFER' with no shipped increment — the roadmap as designed never delivers 'share this row with one user'; the only substitute offered is org membership (F3), which is a different capability.

Field-level rules (gap 3) are covered only on the WRITE side: authz F4's writableBy gates delivery writes per field, but field-level READ rules beyond the existing boolean publicRead (e.g. 'this field visible only to role editor' / claim-gated projection) appear in no design — publicRead remains all-or-nothing public, and no design extends toPublicView with identity-aware projection.

Hosted compute (gap 1) is covered only in name: the compute design (GAP I) is explicitly the ANTI-hosting boundary — signed BYO before-write hooks plus a closed 4-function computed-field vocabulary — and its keyDecisions forbid executing tenant expressions/code in-process. If gap 1 means actual hosted execution, no design delivers it; the roadmap should state the reframe explicitly rather than let GAP I's placement imply closure.

Cross-row transactions (gap 2) are closed only for the agent surface: atomicity B2 makes transact MCP-only by design ('not on the delivery API'). End-user/delivery consumers (e.g. the booking flow from a public site) have no atomic composite write and no design offers one.

Payments (gap 11): payments K covers one-time mode:'payment' Checkout only; subscriptions, invoicing, refunds are explicitly excluded, and member-only commerce is structurally forbidden by K2a's access.read:'public' rule. If the payments gap includes recurring billing, it remains open.

i18n querying (intersection of gaps 5 and 9): media-i18n J bars localized fields from where/orderBy/search/aggregate entirely, and search E has no locale awareness — there is no way to search or filter localized content in any locale, in any design (see also the E×J conflict below).

requiredIf on updates (gap 12): constraints A6 explicitly leaves requiredIf create-only ('its condition is ambiguous against a merged row; revisit with transact'), and atomicity B never picks it up — the required-if constraint is enforceable only at create time across the whole roadmap.
weakSpots: realtime H is best-effort by construction: the inline entry_changes insert is error-swallowed with no transaction (acknowledged: 'near-exact but not guaranteed-complete'), CAS updates that hide a row produce no tombstone, the 2s seq hold-back plus 2s polling makes worst-case latency ~4s+, and Netlify SSE degrades to 8s long-poll. The 'sync primitive' promise rests on clients doing periodic full-list reconciles — realtime is delivered as documented-lossy near-realtime pull.

realtime H's recordChange is wired only to the five lib/entries.ts mutation paths, but sibling designs add mutation paths outside that file: versioning C's trashEntry/restoreEntry/purge (lib/trash.ts), C6's restore_entry_version, and atomicity B's transact cores. Restores (which C deliberately makes emit 'created'+restored:true so consumers resync) and entire transact batches would be invisible to the change feed — directly undermining H's convergence goal.

time-flow G's 'workflows' are single-entry state machines plus delayed single-entry actions; multi-entry orchestration is explicitly excluded in the updated BOUNDARIES, and actor gating is type-granular only ('admin' includes client-role members, per G's own risks). Gap 7's 'workflows' is delivered in its narrowest defensible reading.

search E's delivery semantic search 422s whenever ANY searchable field is non-publicRead — semantic/hybrid over mixed-visibility content is MCP-only. Additionally, any searchable-set or model change empties/stales all embeddings and semantic search hard-fails with E_INDEX_STALE until an agent manually loops sync_semantic_index (50 rows/call) to completion — semantic downtime proportional to collection size on routine schema churn, with no automation until GAP G is wired in (which E leaves optional).

payments K leaves the audit trail for its most trust-critical path undecided: logging of unmapped/ignored/cross-project-metadata inbound Stripe events is 'decide during K4 implementation' because of the webhook_deliveries.collectionId NOT NULL constraint — the very events that indicate probing or misconfiguration may go unlogged (also see the G3/K conflict).

media-i18n J1's 40-derivative budget pinches legitimate use: 12 ladder widths x 2 formats = 24 single-dim variants alone, so a responsive site serving webp+jpeg srcsets plus a few both-dim crops exceeds 40 in normal operation and starts receiving 429s; the design defers this to 'revisit with real usage data' while making the budget the load-bearing abuse defense.

compute I5's own worst-case math admits impossible configurations: 100 items / concurrency 5 x 5000ms timeout = 100s of synchronous hook calls inside a single serverless request (Netlify ~10s) — the latency is documented in the tool description but nothing enforces item-count x timeout against the host budget.

constraints A4's pattern-violation scan caps at 5000 rows, so tightening warnings on large collections are knowingly partial (scannedRows) with no completion path — the promised 'agent learns this collection has N violating rows' degrades to a sample on exactly the collections where it matters most.

atomicity B5's transact_receipts has no retention mechanism (acknowledged unbounded growth, punted to a future jobs gap that G never claims), and every transact call pays a 100-300ms WebSocket pool setup — both acceptable at dogfood scale but unaddressed in any increment.
conflicts: updateEntryIf pre-image built THREE incompatible ways: atomicity B1 uses an advisory pre-read SELECT and explicitly REJECTS the self-join 'UPDATE ... FROM (SELECT ...) prev ... RETURNING prev.data' as a wrong-pre-image bug under READ COMMITTED/EvalPlanQual; versioning C8 builds exactly that rejected self-join; time-flow G4/G4b builds a third variant ('FROM entries old ... RETURNING old.data') and treats it as standard semantics needing only a smoke proof. B1 and C8 each claim to close the SAME documented gotcha (CAS events lacking previous/changedFields at lib/entries.ts:316) — a double-build with contradictory correctness judgments; realtime H and payments K both consume 'whichever pre-image lands'.

deleteEntry double-refactor with undefined composition: versioning C1 rewires deleteEntry into a trashEntry CTE row-move into entries_trash, while atomicity B2 extracts deleteEntryCore as a hard DELETE with abort-on-missing semantics for transact. After C1, what a transact delete op does — trash the row, hard-delete it, emit which event, write a feed row — is specified nowhere; B's rollback/receipt story never mentions entries_trash.

Write-pipeline choke-point claims drift across designs: time-flow G4's security argument rests on 'only two db.insert(entries) sites exist' (createEntry + bulkCreateEntries), but atomicity B2/B3's transact cores add a third insert path; compute I1's hooks and I3's computed stamping live inside createEntry/updateEntry, yet B specifies its cores as 'the exact same buildEntrySchema + verifyRefs + rethrowUnique' with no mention of hooks, computed fields, or workflow initial-state enforcement — as designed, transact bypasses GAP I hooks and GAP G workflow create rules. I acknowledges transact must run hooks pre-flight; B never reciprocates.

realtime H's write-time vis schema hardcodes the 3-preset access model ({read: 'public'|'authenticated'|'owner', ownerField}) that authz F replaces with ClaimRules, any-of preset arrays, and access.org rowClauses — H's then-AND-now identity gate can neither store nor evaluate F's rules, so landing F after H makes the feed's gating either break or silently ignore claim/org restrictions. Same class of drift: query-power D's assertPublicTarget and payments K2a's "access.read must be 'public' or absent" checks are written against the string-preset model F turns into a union/array; F notes route-level coordination but none of D/H/K model F's shapes.

search E x media-i18n J: `searchable` (E) and `localized` (J) are both knobs on text/richtext fields and NEITHER meta-schema rejects the combination — E's searchVectorExpr (data->>'f') and buildSearchDoc concatenation would index raw variant-map JSON for localized fields, and the GIN index expression would differ from what delivery serves. J flags this only as a cross-gap docs note ('search must skip or locale-scope localized fields'); E's design never mentions localized at all.

Two designs settle the same neon-http no-transactions constraint with different infrastructure: atomicity B2 ships lib/db-tx.ts withTransaction (WebSocket pool) and its crossGapDependencies explicitly offer it so 'the queue gap can reuse it instead of building its own client' (naming FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED); time-flow G1 instead builds its own single-statement UPDATE...IN(SELECT...FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED) claim with an unproven-driver risk and a fallback plan, never mentioning withTransaction.

webhook_deliveries contract contention: time-flow G3 makes collectionId NULLABLE (for schedule fires), while payments K's K4 risk analysis assumes it is NOT NULL and designs logging workarounds around that constraint. Meanwhile FOUR designs extend the same log with url-prefix discriminators (compute I 'hook.*', search E 'embed:*', payments K 'stripe:*', time-flow G delayed actions) but only I specifies a refireDelivery guard — refiring an 'embed:' or 'stripe:' row is unspecified in E and K, and I's 'three-shape log' risk is already stale (it's five shapes).

updateEntryIf is concurrently rewritten by four designs with no ordering: constraints A6 changes its dataExpr to '(data - $nullKeys) || $sets', atomicity B1 adds a guard-projecting diagnostic SELECT, versioning C8 converts it to a self-join, time-flow G4 adds a workflow IN-clause guard AND converts it to a different self-join. Concretely, B1's diagnoseCasFailure enumerates 'every guard the failed WHERE contained' from if-clause + increment knowledge only — G4's workflow guard is a guard class it doesn't know, so a workflow-from mismatch would be misdiagnosed as 'row changed concurrently — re-read and retry'.

Deletion-signaling double-build: versioning C's crossGapDependencies proposes entries_trash rows as the change feed's tombstones ('instead of inventing a tombstone table'), while realtime H independently builds entry_changes kind:'deleted' tombstone rows plus O(entryCount) collection-delete tombstone appends — two tombstone mechanisms with independent 30-day retention sweeps (C2's trash sweep, H1's feed prune) that no design reconciles.
